groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Sender, offer, recipient context, relevance, facts, package, CTA, tone, length.

groups.title: Recipient & Personalization
groups.description: Named contact details, personalization, relationship, and contact source.

groups.title: Offer & Evidence
groups.description: Differentiator, proof, links, next-step details, pricing, objections.

groups.title: Delivery, Compliance & Output
groups.description: Region, sender details, opt-out, regulated topics, variants, extra rules.

variables.label: Sender name and company
variables.placeholder: Alex Morgan, Partnerships Lead at Northstar Labs
variables.hint: Enter the sender’s real name, role, company, or other accurate professional identity.

help.why: The email must clearly identify who is contacting the recipient.
help.example: Alex Morgan, Partnerships Lead at Northstar Labs
help.avoid: Fake names, invented titles, or hidden sender identity.

variables.label: What are you offering?
variables.placeholder: Describe the product, service, partnership, opportunity, or request and the outcome it may provide.
variables.hint: Explain the offer in plain language and focus on the recipient-relevant outcome.

help.why: The model cannot safely infer the actual offer or the value it may provide.
help.example: A lightweight support triage workflow that may reduce ticket backlog for small CS teams
help.avoid: Guaranteed ROI or vague “we help companies grow”.

variables.label: Primary outreach goal
variables.placeholder: Select the desired outcome
variables.hint: Choose the single main action the outreach should encourage.

help.why: The goal determines the CTA and how directly the email should present the offer.
help.example: Start a conversation
help.avoid: Stacking meeting + referral + purchase in one email.

options: Invite the recipient to an event

variables.label: Target recipient
variables.placeholder: Example: Heads of customer support at B2B SaaS companies with 20–100 employees
variables.hint: Describe the recipient’s role, company type, situation, and relevant level of responsibility.

help.why: The message must reflect the recipient’s likely context rather than address a generic audience.
help.example: Heads of customer support at B2B SaaS companies with 20–100 employees
help.avoid: Generic “decision makers everywhere”.

variables.label: Why is this relevant to them?
variables.placeholder: Explain the real reason this recipient or segment may care about the message.
variables.hint: Use genuine business relevance, a public trigger, a shared context, or a clearly matched need.

help.what: Why is this relevant to them?.
help.why: Cold outreach requires a legitimate reason for contacting this particular recipient or segment.
help.example: They recently posted about hiring support agents and ticket volume
help.avoid: Fabricated research into private company problems.

variables.label: Approved facts and claims
variables.placeholder: List product capabilities, results, pricing, dates, clients, metrics, or limitations that may be stated.
variables.hint: Include only information that is accurate and approved for external use.

help.why: It establishes the factual boundaries of the outreach and prevents invented claims.
help.example: Used by X customers; Early Access; Free 5/day — only if true
help.avoid: Invented metrics, clients, or capabilities.

variables.label: Email package
variables.placeholder: Select an outreach package
variables.hint: Choose how many emails should be included in the generated package.

help.why: The required structure changes materially depending on whether follow-ups are requested.
help.example: Initial email + 2 follow-ups
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Preferred CTA
variables.placeholder: Select a CTA style
variables.hint: Auto chooses the lowest-friction action that supports the outreach goal.

help.why: Some campaigns require a particular next step, while others benefit from automatic selection.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

options: Invite them to view a resource
Use my CTA from the context

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: English
variables.hint: Enter the language or regional language variant for the generated emails.

help.why: The requested language cannot always be inferred from the offer or recipient information.
help.example: English
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Tone
variables.placeholder: Select the desired tone
variables.hint: Choose the overall voice without prescribing every sentence.

help.why: Tone materially changes how the sender and offer are perceived.
help.example: Clear and natural
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Email length
variables.placeholder: Select a preferred length
variables.hint: The model will stay within the corresponding recommended word range.

help.why: Cold email length varies meaningfully by offer complexity and recipient seniority.
help.example: Concise
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Recipient name
variables.placeholder: Jordan
variables.hint: Leave blank when creating a reusable email for a recipient segment.

help.why: It enables a recipient-ready draft when the specific name is known.
help.example: Jordan
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Recipient company
variables.placeholder: Example Company
variables.hint: Use the accurate organization name only when it is known.

help.why: Company context may materially improve relevance and message specificity.
help.example: Example Company
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Recipient role
variables.placeholder: VP of Customer Success
variables.hint: Enter the real title or a clearly described responsibility.

help.why: A known role helps adapt the benefits, vocabulary, and CTA to the recipient’s authority.
help.example: VP of Customer Success
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Personalization details
variables.placeholder: Recent launch, published article, hiring activity, public statement, technology used, or shared context.
variables.hint: Add only verifiable details that may be referenced naturally in the email.

help.why: It supports genuine personalization without encouraging fabricated familiarity.
help.example: Public LinkedIn post about onboarding bottlenecks
help.avoid: Pretending private knowledge or false familiarity.

variables.label: Likely priority or challenge
variables.placeholder: Describe a relevant business priority without claiming to know private problems.
variables.hint: Frame uncertain challenges as possibilities, not as established facts.

help.why: A supplied priority can connect the offer to a meaningful recipient concern.
help.example: Describe a relevant business priority without claiming to know private problems.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Relationship basis
variables.placeholder: Select the relationship context
variables.hint: The model will not imply a stronger relationship than the one selected.

help.why: The opening and compliance treatment depend on the real relationship between the parties.
help.example: No prior relationship
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Referral details
variables.placeholder: Who referred you, what they said, and whether their name may be mentioned.
variables.hint: Do not mention a referral unless the details are accurate and permission is appropriate.

help.why: A referral-based opening requires exact information and permission boundaries.
help.example: Introduced by Sam Lee — may mention first name only
help.avoid: Name-dropping without permission or accuracy.

variables.label: How was the contact found?
variables.placeholder: Company website, public directory, event list, existing CRM, referral, or another lawful source.
variables.hint: Describe the source without pasting unnecessary personal or sensitive data.

help.why: The source can affect privacy considerations and whether a personalization detail is appropriate to use.
help.example: Company website, public directory, event list, existing CRM, referral, or another lawful source.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Why choose this offer?
variables.placeholder: Explain the most relevant difference without unsupported superiority claims.
variables.hint: Focus on one or two meaningful distinctions rather than a full feature list.

help.why: A specific differentiator can make the value proposition more credible and memorable.
help.example: Explain the most relevant difference without unsupported superiority claims.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Proof points
variables.placeholder: Approved results, client examples, credentials, process evidence, or product data.
variables.hint: Include only evidence that may be stated publicly and attributed accurately.

help.why: Verified proof can support the offer without forcing the model to invent social proof.
help.example: Approved results, client examples, credentials, process evidence, or product data.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Supporting links
variables.placeholder: Paste relevant case study, portfolio, product, research, or documentation links.
variables.hint: Links are used only when relevant and are never invented.

help.why: A supplied link may provide a low-friction way for the recipient to verify the offer.
help.example: Paste relevant case study, portfolio, product, research, or documentation links.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Offer or next-step details
variables.placeholder: Free review, sample, trial, consultation, event date, resource, or proposed next step.
variables.hint: Include conditions, availability, and deadlines only when they are real.

help.why: Some outreach depends on a concrete next step that cannot be safely inferred.
help.example: Free review, sample, trial, consultation, event date, resource, or proposed next step.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Pricing in the email
variables.placeholder: Choose whether pricing may be mentioned
variables.hint: Pricing is included only when an exact approved figure exists in the supplied facts.

help.why: Pricing can either improve qualification or create unnecessary friction depending on the offer.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

options: Mention supplied free option or trial

variables.label: Objections or concerns
variables.placeholder: Timing, budget, switching effort, trust, internal approval, technical fit, or another likely concern.
variables.hint: Add only objections that should be handled briefly and naturally.

help.why: Known objections may change the framing, proof, and CTA.
help.example: Timing, budget, switching effort, trust, internal approval, technical fit, or another likely concern.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Primary recipient region
variables.placeholder: Select the primary region
variables.hint: Used for compliance caution, language conventions, and footer recommendations—not as legal advice.

help.why: Email marketing and privacy obligations differ across jurisdictions.
help.example: Auto or unknown
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Sender website
variables.placeholder: https://example.com
variables.hint: May be used in the signature or as a supporting destination when relevant.

help.why: A legitimate sender destination can improve transparency and provide context.
help.example: https://example.com
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Business postal address
variables.placeholder: Enter an approved business postal address when required for the campaign.
variables.hint: The model will never invent an address and will flag a potentially missing footer element.

help.why: Some jurisdictions or sending policies require a valid sender address in commercial email.
help.example: Enter an approved business postal address when required for the campaign.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Opt-out handling
variables.placeholder: Select opt-out handling
variables.hint: Choose how the draft should account for declining future contact.

help.why: The draft must support an easy decline without inventing an unsubscribe mechanism.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

options: Include no footer, but flag requirements

variables.label: Sensitive or regulated category
variables.placeholder: Select when applicable
variables.hint: Triggers more cautious wording and an additional verification check.

help.why: Outreach in high-stakes sectors requires stricter claim and targeting controls.
help.example: None
help.avoid: Making medical/legal/financial claims without approved wording.

variables.label: Required disclosures or limits
variables.placeholder: Approved disclaimers, jurisdiction, prohibited claims, eligibility rules, or review requirements.
variables.hint: Provide organization-approved requirements; the generator will not invent legal wording.

help.why: Sensitive content may require exact restrictions that the model cannot infer safely.
help.example: Approved disclaimers, jurisdiction, prohibited claims, eligibility rules, or review requirements.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Restrictions and exclusions
variables.placeholder: Claims, words, clients, competitors, topics, promises, or approaches that must not appear.
variables.hint: Use one field for all campaign-specific restrictions.

help.why: Brand, contractual, and compliance restrictions can materially change the final copy.
help.example: Claims, words, clients, competitors, topics, promises, or approaches that must not appear.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Initial email variants
variables.hint: Generate one to three meaningfully different initial-email approaches.

help.why: The user may need controlled variation without receiving an excessive output package.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.

variables.label: Additional context
variables.placeholder: Add any important instructions or context not covered above.
variables.hint: Use only for information that materially affects the outreach.

help.why: It provides one controlled place for relevant campaign-specific information that does not fit another field.
help.example: Add any important instructions or context not covered above.
help.avoid: Invented personalization, fake proof, pressure tactics, or private data.
